mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG CLS63 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M156 engine typically costs £4,500–£8,000 supply only; a reconditioned M157 costs £5,500–£10,000. Used units are available from £3,000–£5,500. A replacement M157 engine costs on average £10,888 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units reaching £11,759. Rebuilt M157 engines start from £6,995. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLS63.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG CLS63 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which generation. The M156 (C219) has well-documented head bolt and camshaft issues. The M157 (C218) is considered more reliable when maintained properly, but early examples suffer from timing chain stretch and cylinder scoring. Both demand diligent maintenance with 5,000–10,000 mile oil changes.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG CLS63?

acceptedAnswer.text: M156: head bolt corrosion (pre-2011 — the most critical concern), camshaft and lifter wear, cam adjuster rattle, oil leaks. M157: timing chain stretch (2011–2012), cylinder scoring (cylinders 1 and 5), oil wicking into the engine harness, turbo coolant pipe leaks, camshaft sensor oil leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG CLS63 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: CLS63 values remain strong — a well-kept C218 is often worth £15,000–£35,000+. A £4,500–£11,000 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example, replacement is often worthwhile. However, given the costs involved, ensure you understand the full picture before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG CLS63 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. C219 CLS63 (2006–2011) uses M156.983; C218 CLS63 (2011–2017) uses M157.981. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M156 head bolts, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The head bolts can be replaced — they are a known weak point. Replacement costs £1,600–£5,000. If the bolts fail and cause head gasket problems, the engine can be destroyed. A preventative head bolt replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,500+). At the first sign of clicking on cold start or coolant loss on a pre-2011 M156, book an inspection immediately.

hero.h1: Mercedes-AMG CLS63 Engine Replacement — Compare Prices & Get Quotes
hero.subHeadline: The CLS63 AMG has worn two legendary powertrains — the naturally aspirated 6.2-litre M156 in the first generation and the twin-turbo 5.5-litre M157 in the second. Both deliver breathtaking performance, but timing chain issues, cylinder scoring and oil leaks are well-documented. Get quotes from UK specialists for reconditioned, rebuilt and used units today.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLS63 AMG engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The V8's complexity and tight engine bay make it a specialist job.

problems.title: M156 Head Bolt Corrosion (C219 — Pre-2011)
problems.symptoms: Clicking noises on cold start, rising oil level, coolant loss requiring frequent top-ups, head gasket failure, eventual engine failure
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£8,000 (if head bolt failure destroys the engine)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: this is the single biggest risk on the early M156

problems.recommendation: The M156's head bolts corrode due to a design flaw. This issue affected early CLS63 units before AMG updated the bolts in mid-2010. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging Mercedes knew about the problem. If you're buying a pre-2011 CLS63, budget for head bolt replacement immediately. Some owners report head bolt replacement costs around £1,600. A preventative head bolt replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full head bolt failure guide

problems.title: M156 Camshaft and Lifter Wear (C219)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or tapping noise from the top of the engine on cold start, metallic rattle at start-up, reduced performance, metallic debris in oil

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear ticking from the top of the engine, have it inspected immediately

problems.recommendation: The M156 suffers from premature camshaft and lifter wear. The camshaft adjusters' locking pins can wear, causing rattle at start-up. If you notice ticking on cold start, have the camshafts and lifters inspected immediately. Regular oil changes with the correct MB-spec oil are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full camshaft and lifter wear guide

problems.title: M157 Timing Chain Stretch (C218 — 2011–2012)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors, eventual chain slip
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the timing chain fails on the M157, the engine is typically destroyed

problems.recommendation: Early M157 engines (2011–2012) have timing chain stretch issues. The problem stems from oil drainage from the cylinder head on initial start-up. Updated non-return valves and revised chain tensioners are available. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: M157 Cylinder Scoring (C218)
problems.symptoms: Misfire, low compression, excessive oil consumption, engine warning light, rough idle, blue smoke
problems.recommendation: The M157 can suffer from cylinder scoring, particularly on cylinders 1 and 5 due to their proximity to the turbos. The issue is more common on tuned cars. If you notice misfire, oil consumption, or blue smoke, have a compression test and boreoscopy performed. Prevention includes regular oil changes with 0W-40 (MB 229.5) and avoiding hard driving before the engine reaches operating temperature.

failureLink.label: Read the full cylinder scoring guide

problems.title: Oil Wicking into Engine Harness (M156 & M157)
problems.symptoms: Various electrical faults, engine warning light, erratic sensor readings, ECU failure
problems.recommendation: Oil can leak from camshaft sensors and wick through the wiring harness to the ECU, causing electrical faults. This affects M156, M157 and other Mercedes V8 engines. Check the engine harness for oil contamination. If you experience electrical gremlins, have the harness inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil wicking guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: CLS63 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential upgrade — far cheaper than a full engine replacement
Catch early; cheaper than a full engine
Labour-intensive but cheaper than a full engine
If severe, replacement is the only option
Replacement — catch before debris damages the engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLS63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * C218 (2013+) with updated M157 components — later cars benefit from improved timing chain components
* C219 (2011+) with revised head bolts — the defining weakness addressed
* Evidence of regular oil changes (every 5,000–10,000 miles) — all AMG V8s are unforgiving of neglect
* Documentation of timing chain work on early M157 cars
* 7G-Tronic transmission service history — valve body issues can occur on higher-mileage cars
avoidIf: * C219 (pre-2011) with no proof of head bolt work — you're buying a known risk
* Any ticking from the top of the engine on M156 — camshaft/lifter wear is likely
* C218 (2011–2012) with no proof of timing chain work — early M157 chain stretch is common
* M157 with misfire codes and no diagnosis — cylinder scoring is possible
* Poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLS63 AMG is a genuine performance icon across both generations. The C219 M156 (2006–2011) is a naturally aspirated masterpiece with well-documented head bolt and camshaft issues. The C218 M157 (2011–2017) is a twin-turbo V8 with its own weaknesses — timing chain stretch (early cars), cylinder scoring, oil leaks, and oil wicking. The M157 is generally more reliable than the M156 when maintained properly. Both engines are complex and expensive to repair — buy on history, maintain on schedule, and any CLS63 will reward you with one of the most engaging driving experiences available.

cta.label: → Compare CLS63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG CLS63 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Engine Number (C219) — Check if head bolts have been revised
2. Cold Start — Listen for rattles from the front/top of the engine (M156: camshaft/lifter wear; M157: timing chain stretch)
3. Service History — including which generation (C219 or C218) and evidence of major maintenance
4. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (M156 head bolt or cooling system warning)
5. Misfire Codes (M157) — Any stored codes? (cylinder scoring warning)
6. Oil Leaks — Any oil under the car? (M156 valve cover gaskets; M157 cam sensors)
7. Oil Level — Is the oil level rising? (coolant mixing with oil — head bolt failure warning)
8. Transmission — Any rough shifting or delayed engagement? (7G-Tronic valve body issues)
